It doesn't matter about the wife, it doesn't matter about the accountant, it doesn't matter about anything but the first 10. Does he like the idea? That's all that matters in the beginning because if he doesn't like the idea, he could think you're the greatest person you have firms, the greatest firm, he ain't buying. Everything matters in the beginning of the sale. I don't mean that in terms of of course rapport, forget talking to anyone, you're actually in the heart of the sale. It doesn't matter yet about the firm, about you, about his wife, not yet, yes it matters later but it matters based on your syntax, your in control and the first thing I gotta know is this, does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? Because if you don't or you're not certain, guess what? I got some more things to tell you, let me tell you why I'm right and you're wrong. Now you don't say it in those terms but that's what's really going on. You're right, they're wrong. It is a great idea, you are the best broker, your firm is the best firm, it behooves him to have a long term relationship with you but you have to handle that, I can't be all at once, one thing at a time. In the straight line we deal with first things first and the first thing is he's gotta love the idea. So if he says yeah it sounds pretty good, do I say exactly? He's like holy Christ, this guy didn't hear me I guess because people can pick up your tonality. So if he says yeah it sounds pretty good, I say exactly, I mirror his tonality. So he says he's at a six, what do I do? I come back at a six and I match him and then I pace him and I pace him and eventually I lead him into the certainty that I want him to be at. Harder is to make the case for yourself and your company. When I say harder, you can always do it but it's just a bit dicier, depending on who you are. So I say to the guy, you don't know me, if you knew me you probably would be saying let me think about it. And 90 plus out of 100 times he'll say yeah that's the truth, if I knew you I'd be buying. One or two or three out of 100 times the guy will say no, no that's not really what it's about, no that's got nothing to do with it. And if he says that I say wait a second John, you need to tell me that if I put you in the taxi car at 14 and took you out at 52 and put you into a union car by the 12 and took you out at 81 and I put you into text service at 6 and took you out at 19 you would be saying Jordan pick me up five times just come on John. He'll say oh well yeah well then I will giggle almost because he's embarrassed. Well yeah well then I wouldn't. He'll almost laugh. The client will laugh back at you because he's embarrassed. So you're now cut to the chase which is the truth of the matter is you don't know me, I don't have the luxury of a track record. Of course yes, you already said the idea, we both know the idea makes sense. We love the idea. Now let me tell you about myself, about how I, Jordan Belfort, can be an asset to you for the long term, what I stand for, how I deal with my clients, what I pride myself on, all the language patterns you're going to learn. And then I have a transition which is and as far as my company goes, right here as far as my company goes and I seamlessly transition to the next ten with a leading company I don't know about what your companies are but you then have language patterns about your company. So now what you've done is someone said let me think about it and you've said let me ask you a question, does it make sense? You've rebuilt certainty with the first ten, right? So check, you then started, you actually went back to yourself, check, you've resold yourself to the guy, you've resold your company to the guy, ten ten ten and then you ask for the order. Now you're in complete control of the sale. Everyone get that concept here? That's called a loop, L-O-O-P and the reason it's called a loop is because on some level what you did was this. You actually went backwards and resold him, you already mentioned things about the company, you already sold him the company once, you actually went back and resold him the company, you've resold yourself, you've resold the company and then you asked for the order again. Everyone follow that? You don't just say to someone, let me tell you what would be idiotic. Someone says I need to think about it and you say smart businessmen don't really think about things for that long. They make decisions and all the information is fresh in your mind. John, give me a shot in ten thousand shares, believe me, you'll be very, very impressed. Have you done anything, have you created certainty in the three tens? No. You're not buying because of the three tens, you're answering the silly objection, you're accomplishing nothing. So what the loop is, a loop is the stuff in the middle. It's essentially these techniques and the language that we use to create certainty which gives us the right to ask for the order again. If someone says they want to think about it and you say smart businessmen don't think about things, give me a shot, you haven't earned the right to ask for the order again. You're a nag, you're annoying him. If someone says I need to think about it and they say oh wait, let me give you some fresh information about the company, about myself and my firm. Like ah, now you have now, give me, you didn't know that. You give them fresh info and you say give me a shot, you won't be sorry. Everyone see the difference? You've given them a reason to change their mind, new information. That process of how you actually go backwards and resell the company, resell yourself, resell the idea, that's called a loop. The first loop is always the same. It's always the same. Matter what they say to you, you just say here what you're saying, what is the idea, it makes sense. Do you like the idea? That's how it starts and it ends after you've said, if I'd been your broker would you be saying yes or say yeah, that's probably true. You say exactly, you don't know me, I'm a track record, blah, blah, blah. You ask, then you say let me introduce myself, as far as my company goes, you say John. Give me a shot, pick up $100,000 and believe me you will not be sorry. Now he's probably going to say, sounds good, John. Can I call you back and then all of a sudden he'll say, he might go from let me think about it to let me call you back in one hour. That will be the next place he goes. He'll switch the objects or it sounds good but I'm not that liquid right now. The way you lower action threshold is this. Typically it's this. Some sort of guarantee. That's why when you see all these infomercials, what are they always doing? You use the money back and say at the end, they don't say at the beginning, you don't lower action threshold in the beginning, you lower it at the end. When someone's close but not quite there, you temporarily lower their action threshold by saying, and guess what? If I'm wrong, you can give it back. It's not everything I said, you can return it and get a refund. That's how you lower action threshold. In the stock market, you can't do that typically. I guess in some investments you can for the most part you can't. The way you lower action threshold is as follows. I mentioned this before but I want to touch on it a bit more intensely now. You make your clients run a different movie. Let me explain to you what that means. Remember I said to you that I was walking through the airport. I saw the guy take a swing of the golf club and me, the low action threshold, I run the movie that says, oh my God, I can imagine myself on the 17th green on Augusta. They're just playing amazing around the golf field and all the wonderful things. I'm a positive thinking guy. I think of all the great stuff. That's the movie. I actually have pictures in my head. I run a movie. I see myself teeing up on the 18th. Wham. Oh, it feels good. I don't run a movie that says, oh my God, if I'm wrong, I'll feel stupid. I remember when I was, my wife said I'm an idiot about that and I felt short of money and they wouldn't return it when I called for the return. I don't do that stuff. I'm like, ah, it's bad. I don't run the negative movie. My father, on the other hand, will run a movie of disaster. If I'm wrong, oh my God, I'll feel like an idiot. I'll be taken to the, then I'll have to be on the phone trying to return the product. They'll probably screw me for that. Then my wife will be mad at me and I'll be in a full of terrible, terrible, exaggerated downside and then when he runs the upside, he says, yeah, I'll probably take it to the thing and it'll suck. Probably won't even worry. He runs a very negative upside film. Everyone get that? That's some of the high action thresholds. They run a negative, a long-winded negative movie and when they project out into the future, they run a very short movie without a lot of emotional movement. They don't get moved emotionally. Does that make sense? Yes? So what you do to lower action threshold is you make them run a different movie and the way you typically do it is say, you know, let me ask you a question. What's the worst that can possibly happen here? Let's say I'm wrong and the stop drops 10% and you lose $4,000. It's not going to put you in the poor house and they'll say, no, probably say exactly and let's say I'm right. It goes up 20 points and you make 20,000. It's not going to make you the richest man, will it? You'll say, no, say exactly. But what it will do, it will serve as a benchmark for future business. It will show you that I'm a guy that's going to put you into the market at the right time and more importantly take you out. It'll serve to build our track record. We're going to base it this time. Next time I'll actually do another base hit with me and then one more base hit. But after three base hits together, I'm going to ask you if you're going to demand that you step up to the plate and let a home run together. Listen, I plan you, that's the beginning, how you actually make them future pace a different movie about if they're wrong, the consequences are very minor. But if you're right, you say, by the way, what I could do if you besides this one trade over the long term, the way of new issues, IPOs, warrant conversions, investment backing deals, I want to be an asset to you and your family and you start piling on the long term benefits, you blunt the short term movie and then you say, why don't we do this? Instead of taking 50,000, start off small, the firm minimum is $25,000. All I'm asking for is that you can be one shot, very, very small, work with the stuff and you actually make them run a different way. Give me a shot, believe me, you will not be sorry. Sound fair enough? And they're like, all right, do it and they'll do it. Now, it's scary how well it works. Pain lowers the action threshold, which is the fifth variable of the straight line. So once we lower someone's action threshold and we still can't get them to close, well, we'll do a secondary pattern for lowering action threshold and we'll add on pain as well. The way you do that, this process is called looping. It's how you move someone down the straight line from the open to the close in these sort of distinct language patterns and you're building certainty for the three tens, you're building certainty and you lower action, so add on, it's a formula, a little bit of this, a little bit of that and you're just looping back and keep going forward, looping, maintaining control of the sale. Now, what comes first, what comes second, the order in which I say it, you move them down the straight line from the open to the close to a 10, 10, 10, I lower their action threshold, raise up their pain and bam, guess what? Someone who is closeable will be closed. That's what the straight line is about. Now just imagine it this way. We have these five variables, right, five. Product U, company, action threshold, pain, five. Look at it like this. Anyone see the movie, The Italian Job? It's a movie about the state crack safes. Remember when the old man, in the beginning Donald Sullivan, he's trying to crack the safe and he listens and he's spinning the knob and he has a click, right? And he spins it the other way, click, click, right? He's a safe cracker. On some level, what you're doing as a salesperson is you're essentially cracking someone's buying combination, okay? And every time you ask for the order, you're clicking the knob and seeing if the safe opens. If it doesn't open, what do you do? You spin the dial. Now what are the five numbers? In a safe, I mean 23 left, 35 right, 16, there's numbers. In buying, there's five numbers. Here are your five combinations to the safe. Before that safe's going to click open, you have to have them to a 10, 10, 10. That's got to be lowered to the point where it's above, this is going to be above, these three have to be above your action threshold and the pain threshold is like the lubricator on it. Every time you ask for the order and run out of the pattern, you're clicking the safe. Does it open? Everyone get that? That's what you're doing. As you take someone down the straight line, essentially you're picking the lock to someone saying yes. Not everybody needs to be at a 10, 10, 10. I could be a 666. Some people need an 898. Everyone's a bit different. Some people, it's like actually it's easy to get them certain, but they have a very high action threshold. It has to be lowered. You don't really know. The truth is you meet someone, you just don't know. When you pick up the phone, I can't say, ah, he's a 789 with a lower. I don't know. So what do I do? I keep running patterns and going down the straight line and testing with saying give me a shot. I might ask for the order seven times and I try to open the safe. He says no, no, no. I keep spitting the dial, fine, that last one. Boom. The safe opens. I got the order. Does that make sense to everybody? Which is why when you're moving someone down the straight line from the open to the close, okay, you're not going to ask for the order once or twice. You have to ask for the order five or six times because you don't know if you've opened, you might have opened the lock already. You haven't tested with asking for the order. So you don't ask for the order once or twice. You ask for it again and again and again, elegantly. Not like give me a shot, give me a shot. Elegantly. You know, give me a shot, believe me, sir. You'll be very, very impressed. Sound fair enough? Now you wouldn't keep saying you'd be very, very impressed. Sound fair enough? Eight times because you sound like a broken record. You have different ways of asking for the order. You might have six or seven different language patterns for asking for the order. Everyone get that?
